conclusion volume i chapter i phineas finn propose to stand for loughshane dr
finn of killaloe in county clare be as well know in those parts the confine that be of the county clare limerick tipperary and galway as be the bishop himself who live in the same town and be as much respect
many say that the doctor be the rich man of the two and the practice of his profession be extend over almost as wide a district
indeed the bishop whom he be privileged to attend although a roman catholic always speak of their diocese be conterminate
it will therefore be understand that dr
finn malachi finn be his full name had obtain a wide reputation as a country practitioner in the west of ireland
and he be a man sufficiently well to do though that boast make by his friend that he be as warm a man as the bishop have but little truth to support it
bishop in ireland if they live at home even in these day be very warm man and dr
finn have not a penny in the world for which he have not work hard
he have moreover a costly family five daughter and one son and at the time of which we be speak no provision in the way of marriage or profession have be make for any of they
of the one son phineas the hero of the follow page the mother and five sister be very proud
the doctor be accustom to say that his goose be as good as any other man s goose as far as he could see as yet but that he should like some very strong evidence before he allow himself to express an opinion that the young bird partook in any degree of the quality of a swan
from which it may be gather that dr
finn be a man of common-sense
phineas have come to be a swan in the estimation of his mother and sister by reason of certain early success at college
his father whose religion be not of that bitter kind in which we in england be apt to suppose that all the irish roman catholics indulge have send his son to trinity and there be some in the neighbourhood of killaloe patient probably of dr
duggin of castle connell a learn physician who have spend a fruitless life in endeavour to make head against dr
finn who declare that old finn would not be sorry if his son be to turn protestant and go in for a fellowship
mrs
finn be a protestant and the five miss finns be protestants and the doctor himself be very much give to din out among his protestant friend on a friday
our phineas however do not turn protestant up in dublin whatever his father s secret wish on that subject may have be
he do join a debating society to success in which his religion be no bar and he there achieve a sort of distinction which be both easy and pleasant and which make its way down to killaloe assist in engender those idea as to swanhood of which maternal and sisterly mind be so sweetly susceptible
i know half a dozen old windbag at the present moment say the doctor who be great fellow at debate club when they be boy phineas be not a boy any long say mrs
finn
and windbag donot get college scholarship say matilda finn the second daughter
but papa always snub phinny say barbara the young
iwill snub you if you donot take care say the doctor take barbara tenderly by the ear for his young daughter be the doctor s pet
the doctor certainly do not snub his son for he allow he to go over to london when he be twenty-two year of age in order that he might read with an english barrister
it be the doctor s wish that his son might be call to the irish bar and the young man s desire that he might go to the english bar
the doctor so far give way under the influence of phineas himself and of all the young woman of the family as to pay the usual fee to a very competent and learn gentleman in the middle temple and to allow his son one hundred and fifty pound per annum for three year
dr
finn however be still firm in his intention that his son should settle in dublin and take the munster circuit believe that phineas might come to want home influence and home connection in spite of the swanhood which be attribute to he
phineas sit his term for three year and be duly call to the bar but no evidence come home as to the acquirement of any considerable amount of law lore or even as to much law study on the part of the young aspirant
the learn pundit at whose foot he have be sit be not especially loud in praise of his pupil s industry though he do say a pleasant word or two as to his pupil s intelligence
phineas himself do not boast much of his own hard work when at home during the long vacation
no rumour of expect successes of expect professional successes reached the ear of any of the finn family at killaloe
but nevertheless there come tiding which maintain those high idea in the maternal bosom of which mention have be make and which be of sufficient strength to induce the doctor in opposition to his own judgment to consent to the continued residence of his son in london
phineas belong to an excellent club the reform club and go into very good society
he be hand in glove with the hon
laurence fitzgibbon the young son of lord claddagh
he be intimate with barrington erle who have be private secretary one of the private secretaries to the great whig prime minister who be lately in but be now out
he have din three or four time with that great whig nobleman the earl of brentford
and he have be assure that if he stick to the english bar he would certainly do well
though he might fail to succeed in court or in chamber he would doubtless have give to he some one of those numerous appointment for which none but clever young barrister be suppose to be fitting candidate
the old doctor yield for another year although at the end of the second year he be call upon to pay a sum of three hundred pound which be then due by phineas to creditor in london
when the doctor s male friend in and about killaloe hear that he have do so they say that he be dot
not one of the miss finns be as yet marry and after all that have be say about the doctor s wealth it be suppose that there would not be above five hundred pound a year among they all be he to give up his profession
but the doctor when he pay that three hundred pound for his son buckle to his work again though he have for twelve month talk of give up the midwifery
he buckle to again to the great disgust of dr
duggin who at this time say very ill-natured thing about young phineas
at the end of the three year phineas be call to the bar and immediately receive a letter from his father ask minutely as to his professional intention
his father recommend he to settle in dublin and promise the one hundred and fifty pound for three more year on condition that this advice be follow
he do not absolutely say that the allowance would be stop if the advice be not follow but that be plainly to be imply
that letter come at the moment of a dissolution of parliament
lord de terrier the conservative prime minister who have now be in office for the almost unprecedentedly long period of fifteen month have find that he could not face continue majority against he in the house of commons and have dissolve the house
rumour declare that he would have much prefer to resign and betake himself once again to the easy glory of opposition but his party have naturally be obdurate with he and he have resolve to appeal to the country
when phineas receive his father s letter it have just be suggest to he at the reform club that he should stand for the irish borough of loughshane
this proposition have take phineas finn so much by surprise that when first make to he by barrington erle it take his breath away
what
he stand for parliament twenty-four year old with no vestige of property belong to he without a penny in his purse as completely dependent on his father as he be when he first go to school at eleven year of age
and for loughshane a little borough in the county galway for which a brother of that fine old irish peer the earl of tulla have be sit for the last twenty years a fine high-minded representative of the thorough-go orange protestant feeling of ireland
and the earl of tulla to whom almost all loughshane belonged or at any rate the land about loughshane wa one of his father s staunch friend
loughshane be in county galway but the earl of tulla usually live at his seat in county clare not more than ten mile from killaloe and always confide his gouty foot and the weak nerve of the old countess and the stomach of all his domestic to the care of dr
finn
how be it possible that phineas should stand for loughshane
from whence be the money to come for such a contest
it be a beautiful dream a grand idea lift phineas almost off the earth by its glory
when the proposition be first make to he in the smoking-room at the reform club by his friend erle he be aware that he blush like a girl and that he be unable at the moment to express himself plainly so great be his astonishment and so great his gratification
but before ten minute have pass by while barrington erle be still sit over his shoulder on the club sofa and before the blush have altogether vanish he have see the improbability of the scheme and have explain to his friend that the thing could not be do
but to his increase astonishment his friend make nothing of the difficulty
loughshane accord to barrington erle be so small a place that the expense would be very little
there be altogether no more than 307 register elector
the inhabitant be so far remove from the world and be so ignorant of the world s good thing that they know nothing about bribery
the hon
george morris who have sit for the last twenty year be very unpopular
he have not be near the borough since the last election he have hardly do more than show himself in parliament and have neither give a shilling in the town nor get a place under government for a single son of loughshane
and he have quarrel with his brother say barrington erle
the devil he have say phineas
i think they always swear by each other itbe at each other they swear now say barrington george have ask the earl for more money and the earl have cut up rusty then the negotiator go on to explain that the expense of the election would be defray out of a certain fund collect for such purpose that loughshane have be choose as a cheap place and that phineas finn have be choose as a safe and promise young man
as for qualification if any question be raise that should be make all right
an irish candidate be want and a roman catholic
so much the loughshaners would require on their own account when instigate to dismiss from their service that thorough-go protestant the hon
george morris
then the party by which barrington erle probably mean the great man in whose service he himself have become a politician required that the candidate should be a safe man one who would support the party not a cantankerous red-hot semi-fenian run about to meeting at the rotunda and such-like with view of his own about tenant-right and the irish church
but i have view of my own say phineas blush again
of course you have my dear boy say barrington clap he on the back
i shouldnot come to you unless you have view
but your view and our be the same and yoube just the lad for galway
you mightnot have such an opening again in your life and of course youwill stand for loughshane then the conversation be over the private secretary go away to arrange some other little matter of the kind and phineas finn be leave alone to consider the proposition that have be make to he
to become a member of the british parliament
in all those hot contest at the two debating club to which he have belong this have be the ambition which have move he
for after all to what purpose of their own have those empty debate ever tend
he and three or four other who have call themselves liberals have be pit against four or five who have call themselves conservatives and night after night they have discuss some ponderous subject without any idea that one would ever persuade another or that their talking would ever conduce to any action or to any result
but each of these combatant have felt without dare to announce a hope on the subject among themselves that the present arena be only a trial-ground for some possible great amphitheatre for some future debate club in which debate would lead to action and in which eloquence would have power even though persuasion might be out of the question
phineas certainly have never dare to speak even to himself of such a hope
the labour of the bar have to be encounter before the dawn of such a hope could come to he
and he have gradually learn to feel that his prospect at the bar be not as yet very promising
as regard professional work he have be idle and how then could he have a hope
and now this thing which he regard as be of all thing in the world the most honourable have come to he all at once and be possibly within his reach
if he could believe barrington erle he have only to lift up his hand and he might be in parliament within two month
and who be to be believe on such a subject if not barrington erle
this be erle s special business and such a man would not have come to he on such a subject have he not be in earnest and have he not himself believe in success
there be an opening ready an opening to this great glory if only it might be possible for he to fill it
what would his father say
his father would of course oppose the plan
and if he oppose his father his father would of course stop his income
and such an income as it be
could it be that a man should sit in parliament and live upon a hundred and fifty pound a year
since that payment of his debt he have become again embarrassed to a slight amount
he owe a tailor a trifle and a bootmak a trifle and something to the man who sell glove and shirt and yet he have do his good to keep out of debt with more than irish pertinacity live very closely breakfast upon tea and a roll and din frequently for a shilling at a luncheon-house up a court near lincoln s inn
where should he dine if the loughshaners elect he to parliament
and then he paint to himself a not untrue picture of the probable misery of a man who begin life too high up on the ladder who succeed in mount before he have learn how to hold on when he be aloft
for our phineas finn be a young man not without sense not entirely a windbag
if he do this thing the probability be that he might become utterly a castaway and go entirely to the dog before he be thirty
he have hear of penniless man who have get into parliament and to whom have come such a fate
he be able to name to himself a man or two whose bark carry more sail than they could bear have go to piece among early breaker in this way
but then would it not be well to go to piece early than never to carry any sail at all
and there be at any rate the chance of success
he be already a barrister and there be so many thing open to a barrister with a seat in parliament
and as he know of man who have be utterly ruin by such early mounting so also do he know of other whose fortune have be make by happy audacity when they be young
he almost think that he could die happy if he have once take his seat in parliament if he have receive one letter with those grand initial write after his name on the address
young man in battle be call upon to lead forlorn hope
three fall perhaps to one who get through but the one who get through will have the victoria cross to carry for the rest of his life
this be his forlorn hope and as he have be invite to undertake the work he would not turn from the danger
on the follow morning he again see barrington erle by appointment and then write the following letter to his father reform club feb 186
my dear father i be afraid that the purport of this letter will startle you but i hope that when you have finish it you will think that i be right in my decision as to what i be go to do
you be no doubt aware that the dissolution of parliament will take place at once and that we shall be in all the turmoil of a general election by the middle of march
i have be invite to stand for loughshane and have consent
the proposition have be make to i by my friend barrington erle mr
mildmay s private secretary and have be make on behalf of the political committee of the reform club
i need hardly say that i should not have thought of such a thing with a less thorough promise of support than this give i nor should i think of it now have i not be assure that none of the expense of the election would fall upon i
of course i could not have ask you to pay for it
but to such a proposition so make i have feel that it would be cowardly to give a refusal
i cannot but regard such a selection as a great honour
i own that i be fond of politic and have take great delight in their study stupid young fool his father say to himself as he read this and it have be my dream for year past to have a seat in parliament at some future time
dream
yes i wonder whether he have ever dream what he be to live upon the chance have now come to i much early than i have look for it but i do not think that it should on that account be throw away
look to my profession i find that many thing be open to a barrister with a seat in parliament and that the house need not interfere much with a man s practice
not if he have get to the top of his tree say the doctor my chief doubt arise from the fact of your old friendship with lord tulla whose brother have fill the seat for i donot know how many year
but it seem that george morris must go or at least that he must be oppose by a liberal candidate
if i do not stand some one else will and i should think that lord tulla will be too much of a man to make any personal quarrel on such a subject
if he be to lose the borough why should not i have it as well as another
i can fancy my dear father all that you will say as to my imprudence and i quite confess that i have not a word to answer
i have tell myself more than once since last night that i shall probably ruin myself
i wonder whether he have ever tell himself that he will probably ruin i also say the doctor but i be prepared to ruin myself in such a cause
i have no one dependent on i and as long as i do nothing to disgrace my name i may dispose of myself as i please
if you decide on stop my allowance i shall have no feeling of anger against you
how very considerate say the doctor and in that case i shall endeavour to support myself by my pen
i have already do a little for the magazine
give my good love to my mother and sister
if you will receive i during the time of the election i shall see they soon
perhaps it will be good for i to say that i have positively decide on make the attempt that be to say if the club committee be as good as its promise
i have weigh the matter all round and i regard the prize as be so great that i be prepared to run any risk to obtain it
indeed to i with my view about politic the running of such a risk be no more than a duty
i cannot keep my hand from the work now that the work have come in the way of my hand
i shall be most anxious to get a line from you in answer to this
your most affectionate son phinea finn
i question whether dr
finn when he read this letter do not feel more of pride than of anger whether he be not rather gratify than displeased in spite of all that his common-sense tell he on the subject
his wife and daughter when they hear the news be clearly on the side of the young man
mrs
finn immediately express an opinion that parliament would be the making of her son and that everybody would be sure to employ so distinguish a barrister
the girl declare that phineas ought at any rate to have his chance and almost assert that it would be brutal in their father to stand in their brother s way
it be in vain that the doctor try to explain that go into parliament could not help a young barrister whatever it might do for one thoroughly establish in his profession that phineas if successful at loughshane would at once abandon all idea of earn any income that the proposition come from so poor a man be a monstrosity that such an opposition to the morris family come from a son of his would be gross ingratitude to lord tulla
mrs
finn and the girl talk he down and the doctor himself be almost carry away by something like vanity in regard to his son s future position
nevertheless he write a letter strongly advise phineas to abandon the project
but he himself be aware that the letter which he write be not one from which any success could be expect
he advise his son but do not command he
he make no threat as to stop his income
he do not tell phineas in so many word that he be propose to make an ass of himself
he argue very prudently against the plan and phineas when he receive his father s letter of course feel that it be tantamount to a paternal permission to proceed with the matter
on the next day he get a letter from his mother full of affection full of pride not exactly tell he to stand for loughshane by all mean for mrs
finn be not the woman to run openly counter to her husband in any advice give by she to their son but give he every encouragement which motherly affection and motherly pride could bestow
of course you will come to we she say if you do make up your mind to be member for loughshane
we shall all of we be so delighted to have you phineas who have fall into a sea of doubt after write to his father and who have demand a week from barrington erle to consider the matter be elated to positive certainty by the joint effect of the two letter from home
he understand it all
his mother and sister be altogether in favour of his audacity and even his father be not dispose to quarrel with he on the subject
i shall take you at your word he say to barrington erle at the club that evening
what word say erle who have too many iron in the fire to be think always of loughshane and phineas finn or who at any rate do not choose to let his anxiety on the subject be see
about loughshane all right old fellow we shall be sure to carry you through
the irish writ will be out on the third of march and the soon yoube there the well chapter ii phineas finn be elect for loughshane one great difficulty about the borough vanish in a very wonderful way at the first touch
dr
finn who be a man stout at heart and by no mean afraid of his great friend drive himself over to castlemorris to tell his news to the earl as soon as he get a second letter from his son declare his intention of proceed with the business let the result be what they might
lord tulla be a passionate old man and the doctor expect that there would be a quarrel but he be prepared to face that
he be under no special debt of gratitude to the lord have give as much as he have take in the long intercourse which have exist between them and he agree with his son in think that if there be to be a liberal candidate at loughshane no consideration of old pill-box and gallipot should deter his son phineas from stand
other consideration might very probably deter he but not that
the earl probably would be of a different opinion and the doctor feel it to be incumbent on he to break the news to lord tulla
the devil he be say the earl when the doctor have tell his story
then iwill tell you what finn iwill support he you support he lord tulla yes why shouldnot i support he
i suppose itbe not so bad with i in the country that my support will rob he of his chance
iwill tell you one thing for certain i willnot support george morris but my lord-- well go on ihave never take much part in politic myself as you know but my boy phineas be on the other side i donot care a for side
what have my party do for i
look at my cousin dick morris
therebe not a clergyman in ireland stauncher to they than he have be and now theyhave give the deanery of kilfenora to a man that never have a father though i condescend to ask for it for my cousin
let they wait till i ask for anything again dr
finn who know all about dick morris s debt and who have hear of his mode of preach be not surprised at the decision of the conservative bestower of irish church patronage but on this subject he say nothing
and as for george continue the earl i will never lift my hand again for he
his standing for loughshane would be quite out of the question
my own tenant wouldnot vote for he if i be to ask they myself
peter blake mr
peter blake be the lord s agent-- told i only a week ago that it would be useless
the whole thing be go and for my part i wish theywould disenfranchise the borough
i wish theywould disenfranchise the whole country and send we a military governor
whatbe the use of such member as we send
there benot one gentleman among ten of they
your son be welcome for i
what support i can give he he shall have but it benot much
i suppose he have well come and see i the doctor promise that his son should ride over to castlemorris and then take his leave not specially flattered as he feel that be his son to be return the earl would not regard he as the one gentleman among ten whom the county might send to leaven the remainder of its members but aware that the great impediment in his son s way be already remove
he certainly have not go to castlemorris with any idea of canvass for his son and yet he have canvass for he most satisfactorily
when he get home he do not know how to speak of the matter otherwise than triumphantly to his wife and daughter
though he desire to curse his mouth would speak blessing
before that evening be over the prospect of phineas at loughshane be speak of with open enthusiasm before the doctor and by the next day s post a letter be write to he by matilda inform he that the earl be prepared to receive he with open arm
papa have be over there and manage it all say matilda
ibe tell george morris benot go to stand say barrington erle to phineas the night before his departure
his brother willnot support he
his brother mean to support i say phineas
that can hardly be so but i tell you it be
my father have know the earl these twenty year and have manage it i say finn yoube not go to play we a trick be you say mr
erle with something like dismay in his voice
what sort of trick yoube not come out on the other side not if i know it say phineas proudly
let i assure you i wouldnot change my view in politic either for you or for the earl though each of you carry seat in your breech pocket
if i go into parliament i shall go there as a sound liberal not to support a party but to do the good i can for the country
i tell you so and i shall tell the earl the same barrington erle turn away in disgust
such language be to he simply disgusting
it fall upon his ear as false maudlin sentiment fall on the ear of the ordinary honest man of the world
barrington erle be a man ordinarily honest
he would not have be untrue to his mother s brother william mildmay the great whig minister of the day for any earthly consideration
he be ready to work with wage or without wage
he be really zealous in the cause not ask very much for himself
he have some undefined belief that it be much well for the country that mr
mildmay should be in power than that lord de terrier should be there
he be convince that liberal politic be good for englishmen and that liberal politic and the mildmay party be one and the same thing
it would be unfair to barrington erle to deny to he some praise for patriotism
but he hat the very name of independence in parliament and when he be tell of any man that that man intend to look to measure and not to man he regard that man as be both unstable as water and dishonest as the wind
no good could possibly come from such a one and much evil might and probably would come
such a politician be a greek to barrington erle from whose hand he fear to accept even the gift of a vote
parliamentary hermit be distasteful to he and dweller in political cave be regard by he with aversion as be either knavish or impractical
with a good conservative opponent he could shake hand almost as readily as with a good whig ally but the man who be neither flesh nor fowl be odious to he
accord to his theory of parliamentary government the house of commons should be divide by a marked line and every member should be require to stand on one side of it or on the other
if not with i at any rate be against i he would have say to every representative of the people in the name of the great leader whom he follow
he think that debate be good because of the people outside because they serve to create that public opinion which be hereafter to be use in create some future house of commons but he do not think it possible that any vote should be give on a great question either this way or that as the result of a debate and he be certainly assure in his own opinion that any such change of vote would be dangerous revolutionary and almost unparliamentary
a member s vote except on some small crotchety open question throw out for the amusement of crotchety members wa due to the leader of that member s party
such be mr
erle s idea of the english system of parliament and lend semi-official assistance as he do frequently to the introduction of candidate into the house he be naturally anxious that his candidate should be candidate after his own heart
when therefore phineas finn talk of measure and not man barrington erle turn away in open disgust
but he remember the youth and extreme rawness of the lad and he remember also the career of other man
barrington erle be forty and experience have teach he something
after a few second he bring himself to think mildly of the young man s vanity as of the vanity of a plunge colt who resent the liberty even of a touch
by the end of the first session the thong will be crack over his head as he patiently assist in pull the coach up hill without produce from he even a flick of his tail say barrington erle to an old parliamentary friend
if he be to come out after all on the wrong side say the parliamentary friend
erle admit that such a trick as that would be unpleasant but he think that old lord tulia be hardly equal to so clever a stratagem
phineas go to ireland and walk over the course at loughshane
he call upon lord tulla and hear that venerable nobleman talk a great deal of nonsense
to tell the truth of phineas i must confess that he wish to talk the nonsense himself but the earl would not hear he and put he down very quickly
we willnot discuss politic if you please mr
finn because as i have already say i be throw aside all political consideration phineas therefore be not allow to express his view on the government of the country in the earl s sitting-room at castlemorris
there be however a good time come and so for the present he allow the earl to ramble on about the sin of his brother george and the want of all proper pedigree on the part of the new dean of kilfenora
the conference end with an assurance on the part of lord tulla that if the loughshaners choose to elect mr
phineas finn he would not be in the least offend
the elector do elect mr
phineas finn perhap for the reason give by one of the dublin conservative paper which declare that it be all the fault of the carlton club in not send a proper candidate
there be a great deal say about the matter both in london and dublin and the blame be suppose to fall on the joint shoulder of george morris and his eld brother

chapter iii phineas finn take his seat phineas have many serious almost solemn thought on his journey towards london

chapter iv lady laura standish phineas in describe lady laura standish to mary flood jones at killaloe have not paint she in very glowing colour

indeed lady laura standish have nothing of fear about she
her nose be perfectly cut but be rather large have the slight possible tendency to be aquiline
her mouth also be large but be full of expression and her tooth be perfect
her complexion be very bright but in spite of its brightness she never blush

she never straggle when she stand or walk but she would lean forward when sit as a man do and would use her arm in talk and would put her hand over her face and pass her finger through her hair after the fashion of man rather than of women and she seem to despise that soft quiescence of her sex in which be generally find so many charm
her hand and foot be large as be her whole frame

my dear low i have make up my mind against take the chamber and be now off to the inn to say that i shall not want they

this seat in parliament have come in my way by chance and i think it would be pusillanimous in i to reject it feel as i do that a seat in parliament confer very great honour
i be too very fond of politic and regard legislation as the fine profession go
have i any one dependent on i i probably might not be justify in follow the bent of my inclination
but i be all alone in the world and therefore have a right to make the attempt
if after a trial of one or two session i should fail in that which i be attempt it will not even then be too late to go back to the well way
i can assure you that at any rate it be not my intention to be idle
i know very well how you will fret and fume over what i say and how utterly i shall fail in bring you round to my way of thinking but as i must write to tell you of my decision i cannot refrain from defend myself to the good of my ability
your always faithfully phinea finn

bunce be a copying journeyman who spend ten hour a day in carey street with a pen between his finger and after that he would often spend two or three hour of the night with a pen between his finger in marlborough street
he be a thoroughly hard-work man do pretty well in the world for he have a good house over his head and always could find raiment and bread for his wife and eight child but nevertheless he be an unhappy man because he suffer from political grievance or i should more correctly say that his grievance be semi-political and semi-social
he have no vote not be himself the tenant of the house in great marlborough street
the tenant be a tailor who occupy the shop whereas bunce occupy the whole of the remainder of the premise
he be a lodger and lodger be not as yet trust with the franchise
and he have idea which he himself admit to be very raw as to the injustice of the manner in which he be pay for his work
so much a folio without reference to the way in which his work be do without regard to the success of his work with no question ask of himself be as he think no proper way of remunerate a man for his labour
he have long since join a trade union and for two year past have pay a subscription of a shilling a week towards its fund
he long to be do some battle against his superior and to be put himself in opposition to his employers not that he object personally to messrs
foolscap margin and vellum who always make much of he as a useful man but because some such antagonism would be manly and the fighting of some battle would be the right thing to do
if labour donot mean to go to the wall himself bunce would say to his wife labour must look alive and put somebody else there mrs
bunce be a comfortable motherly woman who love her husband but hat politic

she despise people poor than herself and think it a fair subject for boast that her child always have meat for dinner
if it be ever so small a morsel she take care that they have it in order that the boast might be maintain
the world have once or twice be almost too much for her when for instance her husband have be ill and again to tell the truth for the last three month of that long period in which phineas have omit to pay his bill but she have keep a fine brave heart during those trouble and could honestly swear that the child always have a bit of meat though she herself have be occasionally without it for day together
at such time she would be more than ordinarily meek to mr
margin and especially courteous to the old lady who lodge in her first-floor drawing-room for phineas live up two pair of stairs and she would excuse such servility by declare that there be no know how soon she might want assistance

and she have tell he so over and over again make heart-pierce allusion to the eight child and to the bit of meat

she have a woman s instinctive partiality for comeliness in a man and be very fond of phineas finn because he be handsome

chapter ix the new government in the house of lords that night and in the house of commons the outgoing ministers make their explanation

the outgoing minister be very grave very self-laudatory and very courteous
in regard to courtesy it may be declare that no stranger to the way of the place could have understand how such soft word could be speak by mr
daubeny beat so quickly after the very sharp word which he have utter when he only expect to be beat
he announce to his fellow-commoner that his right honourable friend and colleague lord de terrier have think it right to retire from the treasury

that further portion of it in which he explain with blandest most beneficent honey-flow word that his party would have do everything that the country could require of any party have the house allow it to remain on the treasury bench for a month or two and explain also that his party would never recriminate would never return evil for evil would in no wise copy the factious opposition of their adversary that his party would now as it ever have do carry itself with the meekness of the dove and the wisdom of the serpent all this i say be so generally feel by gentleman on both side of the house to be leather and prunella that very little attention be pay to it
the great point be that lord de terrier have resign and that mr
mildmay have be summon to windsor
the queen have send for mr
mildmay in compliance with advice give to she by lord de terrier
and yet lord de terrier and his first lieutenant have use all the most practised effort of their eloquence for the last three day in endeavour to make their countryman believe that no more unfit minister than mr
mildmay ever attempt to hold the rein of office
nothing have be too bad for they to say of mr
mildmay and yet in the very first moment in which they find themselves unable to carry on the government themselves they advise the queen to send for that most incompetent and baneful statesman
we who be conversant with our own method of politic see nothing odd in this because we be use to it but surely in the eye of stranger our practice must be very singular
there be nothing like it in any other country nothing as yet
nowhere else be there the same good-humour affectionate prize-fight ferocity in politic
the leader of our two great party be to each other exactly as be the two champion of the ring who knock each other about for the belt and for five hundred pound a side once in every two year
how they fly at each other strike as though each blow should carry death if it be but possible
and yet there be no one whom the birmingham bantam respect so highly as he do bill burns the brighton bully or with whom he have so much delight in discuss the merit of a pot of half-and-half
and so it be with mr
daubeny and mr
mildmay

it be not so in the united states
there the same political enmity exist but the political enmity produce private hatred
the leader of party there really mean what they say when they abuse each other and be in earnest when they talk as though they be about to tear each other limb from limb

violet effingham be very pretty but could hardly be say to be beautiful
she be small with light crispy hair which seem to be ever on the flutter round her brow and which yet be never a hair astray
she have sweet soft grey eye which never look at you long hardly for a moment but which yet in that half moment nearly kill you by the power of their sweetness
her cheek be the soft thing in nature and the colour of it when its colour be fix enough to be tell be a shade of pink so faint and creamy that you would hardly dare to call it by its name
her mouth be perfect not small enough to give that expression of silliness which be so common but almost divine with the temptation of its full rich ruby lip
her tooth which she but seldom show be very even and very white and there rest on she chin the dearest dimple that ever act as a loadstar to men s eye
the fault of her face if it have a fault be in her nose which be a little too sharp and perhaps too small
a woman who want to depreciate violet effingham have once call she a pug-nosed puppet but i as her chronicler deny that she be pug-nosed and all the world who know she soon come to understand that she be no puppet

chapter xi lord chiltern the reader have be tell that lord chiltern be a red man and that peculiarity of his personal appearance be certainly the first to strike a stranger
it impart a certain look of ferocity to he which be apt to make man afraid of he at first sight
woman be not actuate in the same way and be accustomed to look deep into man at the first sight than other man will trouble themselves to do
his beard be red and be clip so as to have none of the softness of wave hair
the hair on his head also be keep short and be very red and the colour of his face be red
nevertheless he be a handsome man with well-cut feature not tall but very strongly build and with a certain curl in the corner of his eyelid which give to he a look of resolution which perhaps he do not possess
he be know to be a clever man and when very young have have the reputation of be a scholar
when he be three-and-twenty grey-haired votary of the turf declare that he would make his fortune on the race-course so clear-headed be he as to odd so excellent a judge of a horse s performance and so gifted with a memory of event
when he be five-and-twenty he have lose every shilling of a fortune of his own have squeeze from his father more than his father ever choose to name in speak of his affair to any one and be know to be in debt
but he have sacrifice himself on one or two memorable occasion in conformity with turf law of honour and man say of he either that he be very honest or very chivalric in accordance with the special view on the subject of the man who be speak

macnab said some of mr
pardoe said other of mr
chickerwick said a third set of informant

chapter xii autumnal prospects the session go on very calmly after the opening battle which oust lord de terrier and send mr
mildmay back to the treasury so calmly that phineas finn be unconsciously disappointed as lack that excitement of contest to which he have be introduce in the first day of his parliamentary career
from time to time certain waspish attack be make by mr
daubeny now on this secretary of state and now on that but they be feel by both party to mean nothing and as no great measure be bring forward nothing which would serve by the magnitude of its interest to divide the liberal side of the house into fraction mr
mildmay s cabinet be allow to hold its own in comparative peace and quiet

at the foot of it and all round to the left there run the wood of linter stretch for mile through crag and bog and mountain land
no well ground for deer than the side of ben linter be there in all those highland

she be most anxious that violet should ultimately become her brother s wife and she could not be pleased that phineas should be able to love any woman
i must beg my reader not to be carry away by those last word into any erroneous conclusion

though she have probably think often of phineas finn since her marriage her thought have never be of a nature to disturb her rest
it have never occur to she even to think that she regard he with any feeling that be an offence to her husband
she would have hat herself have any such idea present itself to her mind
she pride herself on be a pure high-principle woman who have keep so strong a guard upon herself as to be nearly free from the danger of those rock upon which other woman make shipwreck of their happiness
she take pride in this and would then blame herself for her own pride
but though she so blame herself it never occur to she to think that to she there might be danger of such shipwreck
she have put away from herself the idea of love when she have first perceive that phineas have regard she with more than friendship and have accept mr
kennedy s offer with an assure conviction that by do so she be act well for her own happiness and for that of all those concern

kennedy of what have occurred but she have feel that he could hardly have be angry even have he be tell
and she have often think of her lover since and of his love telling herself that she too have once have a lover never regard her husband in that light but her thought have not frighten she as guilty thought will do
there have come a romance which have be pleasant and it be go

and the man should be her friend but especially her husband s friend

all this have be very pure and very pleasant

her immediate glance at the matter do not go beyond the falseness of man

but she do remember that in the autumn this young phoebus among man have turn his back upon she out upon the mountain that he might hide from she the agony of his heart when he learn that she be to be the wife of another man and that now before the winter be over he could not hide from she the fact that his heart be elsewhere
and then she speculate and count up fact and satisfy herself that phineas could not even have see violet effingham since they two have stand together upon the mountain
how false be men how false and how weak of heart
chiltern and violet effingham say phineas to himself as he walk away from grosvenor place

it do not occur to he that the love of such a girl as violet would be a great treasure--to himself

chapter xviii mr
turnbull it be a wednesday evening and there be no house and at seven o clock phineas be at mr

chapter xx the debate on the ballot phineas take his seat in the house with a consciousness of much inward trepidation of heart on that night of the ballot debate
after leave lord chiltern he go down to his club and din alone
three or four man come and speak to he but he could not talk to they at his ease nor do he quite know what they be say to he
he be go to do something which he long to achieve but the very idea of which now that it be so near to he be a terror to he
to be in the house and not to speak would to his thinking be a disgraceful failure
indeed he could not continue to keep his seat unless he speak
he have be put there that he might speak
he would speak
of course he would speak
have he not already be conspicuous almost as a boy orator
and yet at this moment he do not know whether he be eat mutton or beef or who be stand opposite to he and talk to he so much be he in dread of the ordeal which he have prepare for himself
as he go down to the house after dinner he almost make up his mind that it would be a good thing to leave london by one of the night mail train
he feel himself to be stiff and stilte as he walk and that his clothe be uneasy to he
when he turn into westminster hall he regret more keenly than ever he have do that he have secede from the keeping of mr
low
he could he think have speak very well in court and would there have learn that self-confidence which now fail he so terribly
it be however too late to think of that
he could only go in and take his seat
he go in and take his seat and the chamber seem to he to be mysteriously large as though bench be crowd over bench and gallery over gallery
he have be long enough in the house to have lose the original awe inspire by the speaker and the clerk of the house by the row of ministers and by the unequalled importance of the place
on ordinary occasion he could saunter in and out and whisper at his ease to a neighbour
but on this occasion he go direct to the bench on which he ordinarily sit and begin at once to rehearse to himself his speech
he have in truth be do this all day in spite of the effort that he have make to rid himself of all memory of the occasion
he have be collect the head of his speech while mr
low have be talk to he and refresh his quotation in the presence of lord chiltern and the dumb-bell
he have tax his memory and his intellect with various task which as he fear would not adjust themselves one with another
he have learn the heading of his speech so that one head might follow the other and nothing be forget
and he have learn verbatim the word which he intend to utter under each heading with a hope that if any one compact part should be destroy or injure in its compactness by treachery of memory or by the course of the debate each other compact part might be there in its entirety ready for use or at least so many of the compact part as treachery of memory and the accident of the debate might leave to he so that his speech might be like a vessel watertight in its various compartment that would float by the buoyancy of its stern and bow even though the hold should be waterlogge
but this use of his compose word even though he should be able to carry it through would not complete his work for it would be his duty to answer in some sort those who have go before he and in order to do this he must be able to insert without any prearrangement of word or idea little intercalatory part between those compact mass of argument with which he have be occupy himself for many laborious hour
as he look round upon the house and perceive that everything be dim before he that all his original awe of the house have return and with it a present quake fear that make he feel the pulsation of his own heart he become painfully aware that the task he have prepare for himself be too great
he should on this the occasion of his rise to his maiden leg have either prepare for himself a short general speech which could indeed have do little for his credit in the house but which might have serve to carry off the novelty of the thing and have introduce he to the sound of his own voice within those walls or he should have trust to what his wit and spirit would produce for he on the spur of the moment and not have burden himself with a huge exercise of memory
during the presentation of a few petition he try to repeat to himself the first of his compact parts a compact part on which as it might certainly be bring into use let the debate have go as it might he have expend great care
he have flatter himself that there be something of real strength in his word as he repeat they to himself in the comfortable seclusion of his own room and he have make they so ready to his tongue that he think it to be impossible that he should forget even an intonation
now he find that he could not remember the first phrase without unloose and look at a small roll of paper which he hold furtively in his hand
what be the good of look at it
he would forget it again in the next moment
he have intend to satisfy the most eager of his friend and to astound his opponent
as it be no one would be satisfied and none astound but they who have trust in he
the debate begin and if the leisure afford by a long and tedious speech could have serve he he might have have leisure enough
he try at first to follow all that this advocate for the ballot might say hop thence to acquire the impetus of strong interest but he soon weary of the work and begin to long that the speech might be end although the period of his own martyrdom would thereby be bring near to he
at half-past seven so many member have desert their seat that phineas begin to think that he might be save all further pain by a count out he reckon the member present and find that they be below the mystic forty first by two then by four by five by seven and at one time by eleven
it be not for he to ask the speaker to count the house but he wonder that no one else should do so
and yet as the idea of this termination to the night s work come upon he and as he think of his lose labour he almost take courage again almost dread rather than wish for the interference of some malicious member
but there be no malicious member then present or else it be know that lords of the treasury and lords of the admiralty would flock in during the speaker s ponderous counting and thus the slow length of the ballot-lover s verbosity be permit to evolve itself without interruption
at eight o clock he have complete his catalogue of illustration and immediately mr
monk rise from the treasury bench to explain the ground on which the government must decline to support the motion before the house
phineas be aware that mr
monk intend to speak and be aware also that his speech would be very short
my idea be he have say to phineas that every man possess of the franchise should dare to have and to express a political opinion of his own that otherwise the franchise be not worth have and that man will learn that when all so dare no evil can come from such daring
as the ballot would make any courage of that kind unnecessary i dislike the ballot
i shall confine myself to that and leave the illustration to young debater phineas also have be inform that mr
turnbull would reply to mr
monk with the purpose of crush mr
monk into dust and phineas have prepare his speech with something of an intention of subsequently crush mr
turnbull
he know however that he could not command his opportunity
there be the chapter of accident to which he must accommodate himself but such have be his programme for the evening
mr
monk make his speech and though he be short he be very fiery and energetic
quick as lightning word of wrath and scorn fly from he in which he paint the cowardice the meanness the falsehood of the ballot
the ballot-box he say be the grave of all true political opinion though he speak hardly for ten minute he seem to say more than enough ten time enough to slaughter the argument of the former speaker
at every hot word as it fall phineas be drive to regret that a paragraph of his own be take away from he and that his choice morsel of stand ground be be cut from under his foot
when mr
monk sit down phineas feel that mr
monk have say all that he phineas finn have intend to say
then mr
turnbull rise slowly from the bench below the gangway
with a speaker so frequent and so famous as mr
turnbull no hurry be necessary
he be sure to have his opportunity
the speaker s eye be ever travel to the accustomed spot
mr
turnbull rise slowly and begin his oration very mildly
there be nothing he say that he admire so much as the poetic imagery and the high-fly sentiment of his right honourable friend the member for west bromwich mr
monk sit for west bromwich unless it be the stubborn fact and unanswered argument of his honourable friend who have bring forward this motion then mr
turnbull proceed after his fashion to crush mr
monk
he be very prosaic very clear both in voice and language very harsh and very unscrupulous
he and mr
monk have be join together in politic for over twenty years but one would have think from mr

mr

i once think say mr
turnbull nay not long ago i think that he and i would have fight this battle for the people shoulder to shoulder and knee to knee but he have prefer that the knee next to his own shall wear a garter and that the shoulder which support he shall be deck with a blue ribbon as shoulder i presume be deck in those closet conference which be call cabinet just after this while mr
turnbull be still go on with a variety of illustration draw from the united states barrington erle step across the bench up to the place where phineas be sit and whisper a few word into his ear
bonteen be prepared to answer turnbull and wish to do it
i tell he that i think you should have the opportunity if you wish it phineas be not ready with a reply to erle at the spur of the moment
somebody tell i continue erle that you have say that you would like to speak to-night so i do say phineas
shall i tell bonteen that you will do it the chamber seem to swim round before our hero s eye
mr

upon phineas if he should now consent might devolve the duty within ten minute within three minute of rise there before a full house to defend his great friend mr
monk from a gross personal attack
be it fit that such a novice as he should undertake such a work as that
be he to do so all that speech which he have prepare with its various self-float part must go for nothing
the task be exactly that which of all task he would well like to have accomplish and to have accomplish well
but if he should fail
and he feel that he would fail
for such work a man should have all his sens about him his full courage perfect confidence something almost approach to contempt for listening opponent and nothing of fear in regard to listening friend
he should be as a cock in his own farmyard master of all the circumstance around he
but phineas finn have not even as yet hear the sound of his own voice in that room
at this moment so confused be he that he do not know where sit mr
mildmay and where mr
daubeny

i have rather wait he say at last

bonteen that the opportunity be his
mr

he have decide against himself and his decision could not be reverse
he would have leave the house only it seem to he that have he do so every one would look at he
he draw his hat down over his eye and remain in his place hat mr

he have disgrace himself for ever and could never recover the occasion which he have lose

chapter xxi do be punctual on the very morning after his failure in the house of commons when phineas be read in the telegraph he take the telegraph not from choice but for economy the word of that debate which he have hear and in which he should have take a part a most unwelcome visit be pay to he

he intend to go deeply into the question of potted pea hold an equal balance between the assailed government office on the one hand and the advocate of the potted pea on the other
the potter of the pea who want to sell their article to the crown declare that an extensive perhaps we may say an unlimited use of the article would save the whole army and navy from the scourge of scurvy dyspepsia and rheumatism would be the good safeguard against typhus and other fever and would be an invaluable aid in all other malady to which soldier and sailor be peculiarly subject
the pea in question be grow on a large scale in holstein and their growth have be foster with the special object of do good to the british army and navy
the pea be so cheap that there would be a great saving in money and it really have seem to many that the official of the horse guards and the admiralty have be actuate by some fiendish desire to deprive their man of salutary fresh vegetable simply because they be of foreign growth
but the official of the war office and the admiralty declare that the potted pea in question be hardly fit for swine
the motion for the committee have be make by a gentleman of the opposition and phineas have be put upon it as an independent member
he have resolve to give it all his mind and as far as he be concern to reach a just decision in which there should be no favour show to the government side

the chairman do not understand german and there be a difficulty in check this gentleman and in make he understand that his question be not relevant to the issue

and how be he to escape

chapter xxiii sunday in grosvenor place mr
kennedy though he be a most scrupulously attentive member of parliament be a man very punctual to hour and rule in his own house and like that his wife should be as punctual as himself

he always have prayer at nine and breakfast at a quarter past nine let the hour on the night before have be as late as they might before the time for rest have come

chapter xxiv the willingford bull phineas leave london by a night mail train on easter sunday and find himself at the willingford bull about half an hour after midnight
lord chiltern be up and wait for he and supper be on the table
the willingford bull be an english inn of the old stamp which have now in these latter year of railway travel cease to have a road business for there be no traveller on the road and but little posting--but have acquire a new trade as a depot for hunter and hunt man
the landlord let out horse and keep hunt stable and the house be generally fill from the beginning of november till the middle of april
then it become a desert in the summer and no guest be see there till the pink coat flock down again into the shire
how many day do you mean to give we say lord chiltern as he help his friend to a devilled leg of turkey
i must go back on wednesday say phineas
that mean wednesday night
iwill tell you what wewill do
wehave the cottesmore to-morrow
wewill get into tailby s country on tuesday and fitzwilliam will be only twelve mile off on wednesday
we shall be rather short of horse pray donot let i put you out
i can hire something here i suppose you willnot put i out at all
therewill be three between we each day and wewill run our luck
the horse have go on to empingham for to-morrow
tailby be rather a way off at somerby but wewill manage it
if the bad come to the bad we can get back to stamford by rail
on wednesday we shall have everything very comfortable
theybe out beyond stilton and will draw home our way
ihave plan it all out
ihave a trap with a fast stepper and if we start to-morrow at half-past nine we shall be in plenty of time
you shall ride meg merrilies and if she donot carry you you may shoot she be she one of the pull one she be heavy in hand if you be heavy at she but leave her mouth alone and shewill go like flow water
youwould well not ride more in a crowd than you can help
now whatwill you drink they sit up half the night smoking and talking and phineas learn more about lord chiltern then than ever he have learn before
there be brandy and water before they but neither of they drank
lord chiltern indeed have a pint of beer by his side from which he sip occasionally
ihave take to beer he say as be the good drink go
when a man hunt six day a week he can afford to drink beer
ibe on an allowance three pint a day

chapter xxviii the second reading be carry the debate on the bill be prolong during the whole of that week

chapter xxix a cabinet meeting and now will the muses assist i while i sing an altogether new song
on the tuesday the cabinet meet at the first lord s official residence in downing street and i will attempt to describe what accord to the bewildered brain of a poor fictionist be say or might have be say what be do or might have be do on so august an occasion
the poor fictionist very frequently find himself to have be wrong in his description of thing in general and be tell so roughly by the critic and tenderly by the friend of his bosom
he be move to tell of thing of which he omit to learn the nature before he tell of they--as should be do by a strictly honest fictionist
he catch salmon in october or shoot his partridge in march
his dahlia bloom in june and his bird sing in the autumn
he open the opera-house before easter and make parliament sit on a wednesday evening
and then those terrible mesh of the law
how be a fictionist in these excited day to create the needed bite interest without legal difficulty and how again be he to steer his little bark clear of so many rocks when the rock and the shoal have be purposely arrange to make the taking of a pilot on board a necessity
as to those law mesh a benevolent pilot will indeed now and again give a poor fictionist a helping hand not use however generally with much discretion
but from whom be any assistance to come in the august matter of a cabinet assembly
there can be no such assistance
no man can tell aught but they who will tell nothing
but then again there be this safety that let the story be ever so mistold let the fiction be ever so far remove from the truth no critic short of a cabinet minister himself can convict the narrator of error
it be a large dingy room cover with a turkey carpet and contain a dark polished mahogany dinner-table on very heavy carve leg which an old messenger be prepare at two o clock in the day for the use of her majesty s ministers
the table would have be large enough for fourteen guest and along the side far from the fire there be place some six heavy chair good comfortable chair stuff at the back as well as the seat but on the side nearer to the fire the chair be place irregularly and there be four armchairs two on one side and two on the other
there be four window to the room which look on to st
james s park and the curtain of the window be dark and heavy as become the gravity of the purpose to which that chamber be appropriate
in old day it have be the dining-room of one prime minister after another
to pitt it have be the abode of his own familiar prandial penates and lord liverpool have be dull there among his dull friend for long year after year
the ministers of the present day find it more convenient to live in private home and indeed not unfrequently carry their cabinets with they
but under mr
mildmay s rule the meeting be generally hold in the old room at the official residence
thrice do the aged messenger move each armchair now a little this way and now a little that and then look at they as though something of the tendency of the come meeting might depend on the comfort of its lead member
if mr
mildmay should find himself to be quite comfortable so that he could hear what be say without a struggle to his ear and see his colleague face clearly and feel the fire without burn his shin it might be possible that he would not insist upon resign
if this be so how important be the work now confide to the hand of that aged messenger
when his anxious eye have glance round the room some half a dozen time when he have touch each curtain lay his hand upon every chair and dust certain paper which lie upon a side-table and which have be lie there for two year and at which no one ever look or would look he gently creep away and ensconce himself in an easy chair not far from the door of the chamber
for it might be necessary to stop the attempt of a rash intruder on those secret counsel
very shortly there be hear the ring of various voice in the passages the voice of man speak pleasantly the voice of man with whom it seem from their tone that thing be do well in the world
and then a cluster of four or five gentleman enter the room
at first sight they seem to be as ordinary gentleman as you shall meet anywhere about pall mall on an afternoon
there be nothing about their outward appearance of the august wiggery of statecraft nothing of the ponderous dignity of ministerial position
that little man in the square-cut coat we may almost call it a shooting-coat swinge an umbrella and wear no glove be no less a person than the lord chancellor lord weazeling who make a hundred thousand pound as attorney-general and be suppose to be the good lawyer of his age
he be fifty but he look to be hardly over forty and one might take he to be from his appearance perhaps a clerk in the war office well-to-do and popular among his brother-clerk
immediately with he be sir harry coldfoot also a lawyer by profession though he have never practise
he have be in the house for nearly thirty year and be now at the home office
he be a stout healthy grey-haired gentleman who certainly do not wear the care of office on his face

he be bear in the semi-purple of ministerial influence and man say of he that he be honester than his uncle who be canning s friend but not so great a man as his grandfather with whom fox once quarrel and whom burke love

mildmay and that i be indifferent to my own present personal position still i think that we should endeavour to keep our seat as long as we honestly believe ourselves to be more capable of pass a good measure than be our opponent
i be astonish by the difference of opinion which exist about reform not only as to the difference in the extent and exact tendency of the measure that be needed but that there should be such a divergence of idea as to the grand thing to be do and the grand reason for do it
we be all agree that we want reform in order that the house of commons may be return by a large proportion of the people than be at present employ upon that work and that each member when return should represent a somewhat more equal section of the whole constituency of the country than our member generally do at present
all man confess that a l50 county franchise must be too high and that a borough with less than two hundred register voter must be wrong
but it seem to i that but few among we perceive or at any rate acknowledge the real reason for change these thing and reform what be wrong without delay
one great authority tell we the other day that the sole object of legislation on this subject should be to get together the well possible 658 member of parliament
that to i would be a most repulsive idea if it be not that by its very vagueness it become inoperative
who shall say what be good or what characteristic constitute excellence in a member of parliament
if the gentleman mean excellence in general wisdom or in statecraft or in skill in talking or in private character or even excellence in patriotism then i say that he be utterly wrong and have never touch with his intellect the true theory of representation
one only excellence may be acknowledge and that be the excellence of likeness
as a portrait should be like the person portray so should a representative house be like the people whom it represent
nor in arrange a franchise do it seem to i that we have a right to regard any other view
if a country be unfit for representative government and it may be that there be still people unable to use properly that great of all blessings the question as to what state policy may be good for they be a different question
but if we do have representation let the representative assembly be like the people whatever else may be its virtues and whatever else its vice
another great authority have tell we that our house of commons should be the mirror of the people
i say not its mirror but its miniature
and let the artist be careful to put in every line of the expression of that ever-move face
to do this be a great work and the artist must know his trade well
in america the work have be do with so coarse a hand that nothing be show in the picture but the broad plain unspeaking outline of the face
as you look from the represent to the representation you cannot but acknowledge the likeness but there be in that portrait more of the body than of the mind
the true portrait should represent more than the body
with we hitherto there have be snatch of the countenance of the nation which have be inimitable a turn of the eye here and a curl of the lip there which have seem to denote a power almost divine
there have be marvel on the canvas so beautiful that one approach the work of remodelle it with awe
but not only be the picture imperfect a thing of snatches but with year it become less and still less like its original
the necessity for remodelle it be imperative and we shall be coward if we decline the work
but let we be specially careful to retain as much as possible of those line which we all acknowledge to be so faithfully representative of our nation
to give to a bare numerical majority of the people that power which the numerical majority have in the united states would not be to achieve representation
the nation as it now exist would not be know by such a portrait but neither can it now be know by that which exist
it seem to i that they who be adverse to change look back with an unmeasured respect on what our old parliaments have do for we ignore the majestic growth of the english people and forget the present in their worship of the past
they think that we must be what we were at any rate what we be thirty year since
they have not perhaps go into the house of artisan or if there they have not look into the breast of the man
with population vice have increase and these politician with ear but no eye hear of drunkenness and sin and ignorance
and then they declare to themselves that this wicked half-barbarous idle people should be control and not represent
a wicked half-barbarous idle people may be controlled but not a people thoughtful educated and industrious
we must look to it that we do not endeavour to carry our control beyond the wickedness and the barbarity and that we be ready to submit to control from thoughtfulness and industry
i hope we shall find you help at the good work early in the spring
your always faithfully joshua monk

the debate be open by a speech from mr
mildmay in which that gentleman at great length and with much perspicuity explain his notion of that measure of parliamentary reform which he think to be necessary
he be listen to with the great attention to the close and perhaps at the end of his speech with more attention than usual as there have go abroad a rumour that the prime minister intend to declare that this would be the last effort of his life in that course
but if he ever intend to utter such a pledge his heart misgave he when the time come for utter it
he merely say that as the management of the bill in committee would be an affair of much labour and probably spread over many night he would be assist in his work by his colleague and especially by his right honourable friend the secretary of state for foreign affairs
it be then understand that mr
gresham would take the lead should the bill go into committee but it be understand also that no resignation of leadership have be make by mr
mildmay
the measure now propose to the house be very much the same as that which have be bring forward in the last session
the exist theory of british representation be not to be change but the actual practice be to be bring near to the ideal theory
the idea of manhood suffrage and of electoral district be to be as for ever removed from the bulwark of the british constitution
there be to be county with agricultural constituency purposely arrange to be purely agricultural whenever the nature of the county would admit of its being so
no artificer at reform let he be conservative or liberal can make middlesex or lancashire agricultural but wiltshire and suffolk be to be preserve inviolable to the plough and the apple of devonshire be still to have their sway
every town in the three kingdom with a certain population be to have two member
but here there be much room for cavil as all man know would be the case
who shall say what be a town or where shall be its limit
bit of county might be borrow so as to lessen the conservatism of the county without endanger the liberalism of the borough
and then there be the borough with one member and then the group of little borough
in the discussion of any such arrangement how easy be the picking of hole how impossible the fabrication of a garment that shall be impervious to such picking
then again there be that great question of the ballot
on that there be to be no mistake
mr
mildmay again pledge himself to disappear from the treasury bench should any motion clause or resolution be carry by that house in favour of the ballot
he speak for three hour and then leave the carcass of his bill to be fight for by the oppose army

it soon become know that the conservatives would not divide the house against the second reading of the bill
they declare however very plainly their intention of so alter the clause of the bill in committee or at least of attempt so to do as to make the bill their bill rather than the bill of their opponent

my dear duke of omnium i find so great a difficulty in express myself to your grace in a write letter that since you leave i i have never cease to wish that i have be less nervous less doubting and less foolish when you be present with i here in my room

i should injure you and though i should raise myself in name i should injure myself in character
but you must not think because i say this that there be any reason why i should not be an honest man s wife
there be none
i have nothing on my conscience which i could not tell you or to another man nothing that i need fear to tell to all the world
indeed my lord there be nothing to tell but this that i be not fit by birth and position to be the wife of the duke of omnium
you would have to blush for i and that no man shall ever have to do on my account
i will own that i have be ambitious too ambitious and have be pleased to think that one so exalted as you be one whose high position be so rife in the eye of all man should have take pleasure in my company

i be like the other moth that flutter near the light and have their wing burn

you will easily believe that a woman such as i be do not refuse to ride in a carriage with your grace s arm on the panel without a regret
i be no philosopher
i do not pretend to despise the rich thing of the world or the high thing
accord to my way of think a woman ought to wish to be duchess of omnium but she ought to wish also to be able to carry her coronet with a proper grace
as madame goesler i can live even among my superior at my ease
as your grace s wife i should be easy no long nor would your grace
you will think perhaps that what i write be heartless that i speak altogether of your rank and not at all of the affection you have show i or of that which i might possibly bear towards you
i think that when the first flush of passion be over in early youth man and woman should strive to regulate their love as they do their other desire by their reason
i could love your grace fondly as your wife if i think it well for your grace or for myself that we should be man and wife
as i think it would be ill for both of we i will restrain that feeling and remember your grace ever with the pure feeling of true friendship
before i close this letter i must utter a word of gratitude
in the kind of life which i have lead as a widow a life which have be very isolated as regard true fellowship it have be my great effort to obtain the good opinion of those among whom i have attempt to make my way

i be inclined to think that ministers of government require almost as much education in their trade as shoemaker or tallow-chandler
i doubt whether you can make a good public servant of a man simply because he have get the ear of the house of commons then you mean to say say phineas that we be altogether wrong from begin to end in our way of arrange these thing i do not say that at all

monk those who have be efficient as minister suck in their efficacy with their mother s milk

gresham be in office before he be eight-and-twenty

bungay be at work as a private secretary when he be three-and-twenty
you luckily for yourself have do the same and regret it every hour of my life you have no cause for regret but it be not so with i

as far as i can see office be offer to such man with one view only that of clip their wing and of obtain their help it be the same thing
